content.text: The Two Core Paradigms for Moving and Transforming Data
Modern data infrastructure runs on the idea of getting data from where it lives to where it can be analyzed — but

you transform that data fundamentally changes your architecture. ETL and ELT are the two dominant answers to this question, and choosing between them has deep implications for cost, latency, flexibility, and scalability.
is the traditional approach: data is pulled from a source, transformed

it lands in the destination, then loaded in its clean, final form. The transformation happens in an intermediate processing layer — often a dedicated ETL server, a Python script, or a framework like Apache Spark.
flips the order: raw data is extracted from the source and loaded
into the destination in its original form. Transformation happens
the destination system — typically a powerful cloud data warehouse like BigQuery, Snowflake, or Redshift — using SQL or warehouse-native compute.
The single letter difference hides a profound architectural shift in where compute lives and who owns the transformation logic.

Before ETL and ELT existed as formal patterns, organizations faced a chaotic problem: operational databases (where transactions happen) and analytical databases (where reports run) needed to share data, but they serve fundamentally different workloads. You cannot run heavy aggregation queries on a production OLTP database without crushing performance. Data pipelines were the solution — a systematic way to move and reshape data between systems.
The ETL vs ELT distinction matters because:
how flexible your transformation logic is
(can you re-run historical transformations with new business rules?).
who can own the transformation layer
(data engineers vs analytics engineers vs SQL-savvy analysts).
In ETL, the pipeline has three explicit, sequential stages that operate outside the destination system.

Data is pulled from source systems. Sources may include relational databases (via SQL queries or CDC — Change Data Capture), REST APIs, flat files (CSV, JSON, Parquet), message queues (Kafka), or third-party SaaS tools (Salesforce, Stripe, HubSpot). The extract step handles authentication, pagination, rate limiting, and partial failure recovery.
Raw extracted data is processed in memory or on a compute cluster. This stage includes:
Cleaning: handling nulls, deduplication, type casting
Normalization: standardizing date formats, currency codes, phone numbers
Business logic: computing derived fields, applying rules (e.g., classifying customers by lifetime value tier)
Joining: merging data from multiple sources into a unified model
Aggregation: summarizing row-level data into metrics
Validation: asserting that the output meets expectations before writing

The clean, transformed data is written into the destination — typically a data warehouse, a data mart, or an analytical database. Only transformed, validated data lands in the destination. The destination never sees raw data.

[Source DB] ──extract──▶ [ETL Engine / Python] ──transform──▶ [Destination DW]
                               ↑
                        Compute happens here
                        (your pipeline server)
In ELT, the pipeline has a radically simplified first two stages, and the heavy lifting is deferred to the warehouse.
Same as ETL — pull data from sources. The extract logic is identical in concept.
Data is written into the destination

, in a dedicated "raw" or "staging" schema. No transformation happens yet. The destination warehouse receives JSON blobs, un-normalized strings, raw timestamps in inconsistent formats — exactly as they came from the source.
the warehouse using its own compute engine. Tools like dbt (data build tool) manage this layer, defining transformations as SQL
statements organized into a DAG (directed acyclic graph). The warehouse's MPP (massively parallel processing) engine runs the transforms at scale.

[Source DB] ──extract──▶ [Destination DW: raw schema]
                                  │
                                  ▼ (transform inside the warehouse)
                         [Destination DW: transformed schema]
                                  ↑
                           Compute happens here
                           (warehouse engine)

ETL Architecture:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Source Systems → [Extract Layer] → [Transform Layer] → [Load to DW]
                    (connectors)      (Python/Spark)    (JDBC/API)

ELT Architecture:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Source Systems → [Extract Layer] → [Load raw to DW] → [Transform in DW]
                    (connectors)     (fast ingest)        (SQL / dbt)
The ETL transform layer is stateful, complex, and needs its own infrastructure. The ELT transform layer is pure SQL, lives inside the warehouse, and benefits from warehouse scalability automatically.
Sensitive data (PII, PCI) can be masked, hashed, or dropped
it ever enters the destination. Useful when the destination is less trusted than the pipeline environment.
Transformed data can be loaded into any destination — relational databases, NoSQL stores, flat files — because the output format is controlled by the transform layer.
Raw data (which is often messy and large) never hits the warehouse. Only clean data is stored.
Python code can apply ML models, call external APIs, use arbitrary libraries, and implement logic that SQL cannot express.
Many older data warehouses have limited compute; pre-transforming in ETL offloads pressure from the destination.
The full fidelity raw data is always available in the warehouse. If business logic changes, you can re-transform historical data without re-extracting it from the source.
Because no transformation happens in the pipeline, data reaches the destination faster, reducing latency between events occurring in source systems and availability in analytics.
Cloud warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) scale compute elastically. Letting them handle transforms means you never need to size your own pipeline servers for peak load.
Analysts and analytics engineers who know SQL can own and modify transformation logic without needing Python/Spark infrastructure knowledge.
Because raw data is always available, debugging a broken transformation means inspecting the raw table rather than re-running an entire extraction.
Modern ELT tooling (Airbyte, Fivetran, dbt) has matured dramatically. Pre-built connectors to hundreds of data sources mean you often don't write extract/load code at all.
Weak by default — raw data in warehouse
Cheap — raw data already in warehouse
ETL is the right choice when:

HIPAA, GDPR, or PCI DSS compliance may prohibit raw personally identifiable information from landing in a shared warehouse. In ETL, you hash email addresses, drop SSNs, or tokenize card numbers before the data ever reaches the destination.
If loading data into a MySQL/PostgreSQL database, a legacy Oracle DW, or any system that lacks MPP compute, pre-transforming in ETL is the only viable option.

Running an ML inference pipeline to score records, calling a geocoding API to resolve addresses to coordinates, or using NLP libraries to classify text — none of these are expressible in SQL. Python ETL handles them naturally.
When the same data must be loaded into a data warehouse, a Redis cache, and an Elasticsearch index simultaneously, ETL lets you shape the output for each destination independently.
Data volume at the source is enormous but the final output is small.
Aggregating billions of raw events down to a daily summary table in the ETL layer means the destination only receives a few thousand rows, not billions.
ETL is the wrong choice when:
Business logic changes frequently and you need to re-derive historical metrics. Re-running the ETL requires re-extracting from the source.
Your team is SQL-heavy and lacks Python engineering bandwidth to maintain transform code.
You are using a modern cloud data warehouse that has abundant, cheap compute.
ELT is the right choice when:
You use a modern cloud data warehouse.
BigQuery, Snowflake, Databricks, and Redshift are specifically designed for ELT workloads. They have the compute and SQL expressiveness to transform data at scale cheaply.
When how you define a "churned customer" or a "conversion event" changes, ELT lets you redefine the SQL transformation and re-run it against all historical raw data instantly.
Raw data loaded immediately means analysts can query it (even in raw form) minutes after an event occurs, rather than waiting for an ETL transform pipeline to complete.
Tools like dbt have created a new role — the analytics engineer — who owns SQL transformation logic. ELT is the natural home for this pattern.
You want to reduce pipeline maintenance burden.
Using Fivetran or Airbyte for the extract/load leg means you maintain almost no custom connector code. You only maintain dbt transformation models.
ELT is the wrong choice when:
Sensitive data cannot land in the warehouse in raw form, even temporarily.
The destination system lacks the compute power to run complex transformations.
Transformation logic fundamentally cannot be expressed in SQL.
How to Implement ETL in Python
A Python ETL pipeline follows a clear structure. Below is a worked example that extracts user data from a PostgreSQL source, transforms it, and loads it into a destination warehouse.

import psycopg2
import sqlite3  # Stand-in for destination DW
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Iterator

# ── EXTRACT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def extract_users(source_conn) -> Iterator[dict]:
    """
    Pull raw user records from the source OLTP database.
    Uses a server-side cursor for memory-efficient streaming of large result sets.
    """
    with source_conn.cursor(name="user_cursor") as cursor:
        cursor.execute("""
            SELECT id, email, first_name, last_name, created_at, country_code
            FROM users
            WHERE updated_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '1 day'
        """)
        columns = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]
        for row in cursor:
            yield dict(zip(columns, row))


# ── TRANSFORM ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def transform_user(raw: dict) -> dict | None:
    """
    Apply business logic and cleaning to a single raw user record.
    Returns None to signal that the record should be dropped.
    """
    # Drop incomplete records
    if not raw.get("email") or not raw.get("country_code"):
        return None

    # Hash PII before it reaches the destination
    email_hash = hashlib.sha256(raw["email"].lower().strip().encode()).hexdigest()

    # Normalize country code to uppercase ISO 2-letter
    country = raw["country_code"].upper().strip()
    if len(country) != 2:
        return None

    # Derive full name
    full_name = f"{raw['first_name']} {raw['last_name']}".strip()

    # Parse and standardize timestamp
    created_at = raw["created_at"]
    if isinstance(created_at, str):
        created_at = datetime.fromisoformat(created_at)

    return {
        "user_id": raw["id"],
        "email_hash": email_hash,       # PII removed — only hash stored
        "full_name": full_name,
        "country": country,
        "created_date": created_at.date().isoformat(),
    }


# ── LOAD ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def load_users(dest_conn, records: list[dict]) -> int:
    """
    Upsert transformed records into the destination.
    Returns the count of rows written.
    """
    if not records:
        return 0

    dest_conn.executemany("""
        INSERT INTO dim_users (user_id, email_hash, full_name, country, created_date)
        VALUES (:user_id, :email_hash, :full_name, :country, :created_date)
        ON CONFLICT (user_id) DO UPDATE SET
            email_hash   = excluded.email_hash,
            full_name    = excluded.full_name,
            country      = excluded.country,
            created_date = excluded.created_date
    """, records)
    dest_conn.commit()
    return len(records)


# ── PIPELINE ORCHESTRATION ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def run_etl_pipeline():
    source_conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://user:pass@source-db/app")
    dest_conn   = sqlite3.connect("warehouse.db")  # Replace with real DW connector

    transformed = []
    dropped = 0

    for raw_record in extract_users(source_conn):
        result = transform_user(raw_record)
        if result is None:
            dropped += 1
        else:
            transformed.append(result)

        # Batch load every 1000 records to avoid memory pressure
        if len(transformed) >= 1000:
            load_users(dest_conn, transformed)
            transformed.clear()

    # Load remaining records
    written = load_users(dest_conn, transformed)

    print(f"Pipeline complete: {written} written, {dropped} dropped")
    source_conn.close()
    dest_conn.close()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_etl_pipeline()
with a server-side cursor avoids loading millions of rows into memory at once.
PII hashing in the transform layer
means the destination never receives raw email addresses.
(1000 records per insert) balances memory usage against round-trip overhead.
) make the pipeline idempotent — safe to re-run.
How to Implement ELT in Python
In ELT, the Python layer only handles extract and raw load. The transform logic is SQL (typically managed by dbt).

import psycopg2
import snowflake.connector  # Destination: Snowflake
import json
from datetime import datetime

# ── EXTRACT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def extract_events(source_conn) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Pull raw event records from the source.
    In ELT we do NOT transform — we preserve the raw payload entirely.
    """
    with source_conn.cursor() as cursor:
        cursor.execute("""
            SELECT event_id, event_type, user_id, properties, occurred_at
            FROM events
            WHERE occurred_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour'
        """)
        columns = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]
        return [dict(zip(columns, row)) for row in cursor.fetchall()]


# ── LOAD (RAW) ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def load_raw_events(dest_conn, events: list[dict]):
    """
    Write raw events into a staging table in Snowflake.
    No transformation — data is stored as-is, including messy fields.
    The 'raw_payload' column stores the entire record as JSON for full fidelity.
    """
    cursor = dest_conn.cursor()

    rows = [
        (
            event["event_id"],
            event["event_type"],
            event["user_id"],
            json.dumps(event["properties"]),  # Preserve raw JSON structure
            str(event["occurred_at"]),
            datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),     # Ingestion timestamp for lineage
        )
        for event in events
    ]

    cursor.executemany("""
        INSERT INTO raw.events (event_id, event_type, user_id, properties, occurred_at, ingested_at)
        VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)
    """, rows)

    dest_conn.commit()
    print(f"Loaded {len(rows)} raw events into Snowflake staging")


# ── PIPELINE (NO TRANSFORM STAGE) ────────────────────────────────────────────

def run_elt_pipeline():
    source_conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://user:pass@source-db/app")
    dest_conn   = snowflake.connector.connect(
        account   = "xy12345.us-east-1",
        user      = "pipeline_user",
        password  = "...",
        database  = "analytics",
        schema    = "raw",
        warehouse = "LOAD_WH",
    )

    raw_events = extract_events(source_conn)
    load_raw_events(dest_conn, raw_events)

    # Transform logic is NOT here.
    # It runs in Snowflake via dbt models (SQL SELECT statements)
    # triggered separately by an orchestrator (Airflow, Prefect, dbt Cloud).

    source_conn.close()
    dest_conn.close()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_elt_pipeline()
The corresponding dbt SQL model that runs

-- models/marts/fct_page_views.sql
-- dbt runs this SQL inside Snowflake against the raw.events table

{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='event_id') }}

SELECT
    event_id,
    user_id,
    event_type,
    properties:page_url::VARCHAR     AS page_url,
    properties:referrer::VARCHAR     AS referrer,
    properties:session_id::VARCHAR   AS session_id,
    occurred_at::TIMESTAMP           AS event_time,
    DATE(occurred_at)                AS event_date
FROM {{ source('raw', 'events') }}
WHERE event_type = 'page_view'
  AND occurred_at IS NOT NULL

{% if is_incremental() %}
  AND occurred_at > (SELECT MAX(event_time) FROM {{ this }})
{% endif %}

The Python layer simply ships raw data to Snowflake. The SQL transformation runs inside Snowflake's compute engine, managed by dbt, and can be modified and re-run at any time against all historical raw data.
Scenario: E-commerce analytics platform at scale.
An e-commerce company processes 50 million order events per day. They need to compute:

A Spark cluster runs nightly. It reads from MySQL shards (8 source databases), joins order, product, and customer tables, applies CLV scoring using a Python ML model, and writes clean aggregate tables into a PostgreSQL data warehouse. Problems: the Spark cluster takes 4 hours to complete, so analytics are 4–12 hours stale. When the marketing team redefines CLV segmentation thresholds, the entire pipeline must re-run, requiring 4 hours plus re-extraction from MySQL.

Fivetran connectors sync raw order, product, and customer data from MySQL into Snowflake every 15 minutes — no transform, just ingest. dbt models run incrementally every 30 minutes inside Snowflake, computing revenue summaries, CLV tiers (in SQL), and session attribution. When the marketing team redefines CLV tiers, a data analyst updates two SQL files in a Git repo, runs
, and the redefined metric is applied to
Because CLV scoring requires a Python ML model (which cannot run in SQL), a lightweight Python ETL job runs daily: it queries the

table from Snowflake (already cleaned by dbt), runs the ML scoring model, and writes scores back to Snowflake as a separate table. The ELT pattern handles 95% of the work; ETL handles the narrow ML use case.
This hybrid architecture — ELT for all standard analytics, ETL for ML-specific enrichment — is the dominant production pattern at mature data organizations today.
Mistake 1 — Transforming in ETL when ELT would suffice.

Teams that adopted ETL before modern cloud warehouses existed often over-invest in complex Python transformation pipelines for logic that would be simpler, faster, and more maintainable as a dbt SQL model. The test: if your transformation can be expressed in SQL, it probably belongs in ELT.
Mistake 2 — Storing only transformed data in ELT.

Some teams implement ELT but delete raw data after transformation to save storage costs. This eliminates the primary advantage of ELT — the ability to re-derive metrics from raw data when business logic changes.
will create duplicate records if the pipeline re-runs after a partial failure. All load operations should be idempotent — running them twice must produce the same result as running them once.
Mistake 4 — No data quality checks between stages.

Pipelines that blindly pass data between stages without row count validation, null checks, or schema assertions will silently corrupt the destination. An upstream schema change (a column renamed in a source table) can cause an ETL pipeline to load empty columns for months before anyone notices.
Mistake 5 — Treating ELT's raw layer as temporary.

Raw tables are often treated as throwaway staging areas and purged aggressively. In ELT, the raw layer is a first-class, permanent audit log of what source systems produced. Purging it removes the ability to re-derive history.
Mistake 6 — Loading entire source tables incrementally without a watermark.
on every run will pull billions of rows each time. Always filter using a watermark — typically

Source systems add columns, rename fields, and change data types without warning. Pipelines with hard-coded column lists break silently. Build schema inference and alerting on unexpected schema changes.
Every pipeline run should be safe to re-run. Use upserts, truncate-and-reload patterns, or incremental watermarks so that re-execution after a failure does not create duplicates or corrupt state.
Separate raw and transformed data in ELT.
(light cleaning, type casting — no business logic),
(business-logic-heavy, analyst-facing models). This makes data lineage and debugging straightforward.

After extraction, assert row counts are within expected bounds. After transformation, assert nullability constraints, uniqueness of primary keys, and referential integrity. Tools like Great Expectations or dbt tests handle this systematically.
Never hard-code date ranges or incremental windows. Pass the last successful run timestamp as a parameter so pipelines can be backfilled or re-run for arbitrary time windows without code changes.
Capture: rows extracted, rows dropped, rows loaded, run start/end timestamp, and any errors. Store this in a pipeline audit table. This data is essential for debugging and SLA monitoring.
Use connection pooling and batch sizes thoughtfully.

For ETL, loading records one-by-one with individual INSERTs is catastrophically slow. Batch writes of 500–5000 rows per transaction are typical for relational destinations. For columnar warehouses like Snowflake or BigQuery, larger batches (10,000–100,000 rows) or file-based bulk loading (COPY INTO) dramatically outperform row-by-row inserts.
Whether transformation lives in Python (ETL) or SQL (ELT/dbt), it must be version-controlled in Git. This enables rollback, code review, and audit trails for logic changes.

Events from mobile devices, IoT sensors, and distributed systems often arrive minutes to hours after they occur. Pipelines must handle re-processing late data without creating duplicates or incorrect aggregations.
concentrate in the transform layer. A single Python process is bounded by CPU and memory of the host machine. As data volume grows, three scaling strategies apply:
Increase CPU/RAM of the ETL host. Effective up to a point, but expensive.
Stream data in batches (as shown in the code example above) to process arbitrarily large datasets with bounded memory.

Replace single-machine Python with PySpark or Dask for parallelized transformations across a cluster. This is the standard approach for datasets that exceed single-machine capacity (typically >100GB per run).

is effectively handled by the warehouse. Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift can execute transformation queries in seconds against terabyte-scale tables using MPP engines that distribute work across dozens to hundreds of compute nodes. The ELT Python layer (extract + raw load) is comparatively lightweight and scales independently. The primary cost control lever is warehouse sizing and query optimization (partitioning, clustering, materialization strategy in dbt).

For large datasets, incremental processing is essential. Full-refresh pipelines that reprocess all historical data on every run are impractical beyond a few million rows. ETL pipelines use watermark-based incremental extraction; ELT pipelines use dbt's incremental materialization, which appends only new records to existing tables rather than rebuilding them from scratch.
— Workflow orchestrator. Defines ETL pipelines as Python DAGs with scheduling, retries, and dependency management. The most widely deployed pipeline orchestrator.
— Distributed compute engine. Runs Python transformation logic across a cluster. Standard for large-scale ETL.
— In-memory DataFrame library. Suitable for ETL at small-to-medium scale (datasets that fit in RAM — typically <10GB).
— Modern Python orchestrators with better observability and testing support than Airflow.
— Python SQL toolkit for database-agnostic connectivity in ETL load steps.
— Managed ELT connector platform. Provides 300+ pre-built source connectors that handle extraction and raw loading automatically. No pipeline code required for the extract/load leg.
— Open-source alternative to Fivetran. Self-hostable with 350+ connectors. Highly configurable.

— The de facto standard for ELT transformation. Defines SQL SELECT models in a version-controlled project, manages dependencies as a DAG, and handles incremental materializations, tests, and documentation.
— Lightweight managed ELT tool for smaller teams.
— Open-source ELT orchestrator built by the GitLab team. Integrates Singer taps/targets for connectors and dbt for transformation.
— Separation of compute and storage. Pay-per-second compute. Excellent SQL support.
— Serverless, pay-per-query. Extremely fast for ad-hoc analysis.
— Cluster-based MPP warehouse. Strong AWS ecosystem integration.
— Lakehouse architecture. Combines data lake storage with warehouse-grade SQL and Spark compute.
Privacy constraints, non-SQL logic, limited destination compute
Modern cloud warehouses, evolving business logic, SQL-native teams
Syncing analytics results into CRM, ad platforms, or apps
Query source data in-place without moving it
Federated queries across sources; not suitable for heavy analytics

Streaming pipelines (Kafka, Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) occupy a different point in the design space — they process data continuously as it arrives rather than in scheduled batches. They are complementary to ETL/ELT rather than replacements: most organizations run both a streaming layer (for real-time use cases) and a batch ETL/ELT layer (for historical analytics).

Because ETL transforms data before loading, sensitive fields can be permanently removed, hashed, or tokenized in the transform stage. The destination warehouse holds no PII. This is a strong compliance posture for GDPR Article 25 (data minimization by design) and HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification. Ensure the ETL processing environment itself is secured — if a Python server handles raw PII in memory, that server must be within your compliance boundary.

Raw PII lands in the warehouse. Access control becomes the primary security mechanism: column-level security, row-level security, and dynamic data masking (available in Snowflake and BigQuery) restrict access to sensitive columns. This is workable but requires disciplined warehouse governance.
Re-running any pipeline stage should produce the same result. Use upserts, not inserts.
Records that fail transformation or validation should be written to a separate error table or queue for inspection and reprocessing, not silently dropped.
For long-running ETL jobs, persist progress checkpoints so a failure mid-run can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch.
If a pipeline that normally completes in 30 minutes has not finished in 2 hours, trigger an alert. Use Airflow's SLA miss callbacks or Prefect's alerting integrations.
Capture and version the schema of source data at extraction time. Alerts on schema drift catch upstream changes before they corrupt downstream tables.

If the transform stage crashes mid-run, partial data may be written to the destination, leaving it in an inconsistent state. Use transactions and atomic batch commits to ensure either all records in a batch are written or none are.
If raw data is loaded with incorrect types (e.g., a

in a field expected to be non-null, or a date stored as a string in an unexpected format), dbt transformation models may silently cast incorrectly or fail. Schema tests in dbt and raw-layer validation before loading are the primary defenses.
Think of ETL and ELT like two different strategies for moving furniture when you renovate your home.
is like hiring professional movers who unpack, sort, and assemble all your furniture in a staging warehouse before delivering only the finished, assembled items to your new home. The work happens
the destination. The destination is always clean, but the staging warehouse is expensive and the delivery is slower.

is like having all your furniture delivered immediately in boxes, directly to your new home. The home has a large garage (raw schema) where everything is stored temporarily. Then you (or a SQL query) go through the garage and organize each item into the right room. If you change your mind about where the sofa goes, you don't need to call the moving company again — the sofa is already there; you just move it yourself.
The warehouse is the garage. The bigger and more powerful the garage (cloud warehouse compute), the less it matters that everything arrived in boxes.
it reaches the destination; ELT loads raw data first and transforms
the destination using its own compute.
ETL is the right choice when data privacy requires PII to be stripped before reaching the warehouse, when transformations require Python/ML logic, or when the destination is compute-limited.
ELT is the right choice when using a modern cloud data warehouse, when business logic evolves frequently and re-processing history is needed, and when the team is SQL-native.
The primary advantage of ELT is raw data preservation — if definitions change, history can be re-derived without re-extracting from the source.
The primary advantage of ETL is control — transformation logic runs on your infrastructure, giving you full power over data shape, privacy, and destination format.
Production architectures are typically hybrid: ELT for standard analytics (Fivetran + dbt + Snowflake), ETL for narrow cases requiring Python ML scoring or multi-destination loading.
Idempotency, incremental watermarks, schema validation, and data quality checks are non-negotiable in both paradigms.
